items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: Impressionism didn't really begin with a new idea about art. It began with a new tube of paint. Until the eighteen-forties, painters ground their own pigments in the studio, so working outside was impractical. Then portable metal paint tubes arrived, and suddenly artists could carry their colors out the door. The Impressionists seized that freedom. They painted en plein air, a French phrase meaning in the open air, setting up easels in fields, gardens, and along riverbanks. Now, why does that matter for color? Because once you paint outdoors, you start to notice something the studio hides. Shadows are not gray or black. A shadow cast on snow at midday actually looks blue, because it reflects the blue of the sky above it. Monet became fascinated by this. He painted the same haystack again and again at different hours, and the colors shifted dramatically from dawn to dusk, even though the haystack itself never changed. What he captured was light, not the object. This led to a key technique. Instead of mixing a muddy brown on the palette, the Impressionists placed small strokes of pure, unmixed color side by side. Your eye blends them at a distance, so the surface seems to shimmer. That insight, that color is created in the eye and depends entirely on light, broke sharply with the academic tradition, which treated an object's color as a fixed, permanent property. The Impressionists were suggesting something almost radical for their time. Color is not something an object simply has. It is an event that happens between light, the surface, and the viewer. And that shift would echo through nearly every movement that followed.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How painting outdoors led Impressionists to rethink color
options.B: The invention of portable metal paint tubes in France
options.C: Monet's lifelong fascination with painting haystacks
options.D: Techniques for grinding pigments inside an artist's studio

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：整堂课的核心是户外作画如何改变了印象派对颜色的理解（颜色取决于光线、产生于眼中）。B是太狭窄的细节（颜料管只是起因的一个细节）。C也太狭窄，干草堆只是一个例证。D是surface_word_trap，用了讲座里studio、grinding pigments等词，但那只是过去的对比背景，不是主旨。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the portable metal paint tubes?

options.A: To prove that Impressionists invented new pigment colors
options.B: To explain what made painting outdoors finally possible
options.C: To describe how studio painters mixed brown on a palette
options.D: To argue that technology matters more than artistic talent

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：教授提到颜料管是为了解释一个技术变化如何使户外作画成为可能（在此之前画家要在工作室里自己研磨颜料）。A是wrong_purpose，教授没说他们发明了新颜料。C是literal_restatement，调色盘调棕色是后面讲技法时的内容，不是提颜料管的目的。D是over_inference，把技术使能拔高成'技术比才华更重要'的论断，教授并未表达。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why does a shadow on snow at midday look blue?

options.A: Because snow contains tiny blue ice crystals inside it
options.B: Because the Impressionists added blue paint to every shadow
options.C: Because the shadow reflects the blue of the sky above it
options.D: Because midday sunlight removes all blue from open fields

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座明确说雪上的阴影看起来是蓝的，因为它反射了上方天空的蓝色。A是topic_adjacent，听起来合理但讲座没说是冰晶造成的。B是wrong_detail，蓝色是观察到的现象，不是因为画家往阴影里加蓝颜料。D是reversed_logic，颠倒了关系——是天空把蓝色反射进来，而不是阳光把蓝色去掉。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how the academic tradition viewed color?

options.A: It believed color was created entirely within the viewer's eye
options.B: It encouraged artists to paint the same scene at many hours
options.C: It treated outdoor light as the true source of an object's color
options.D: It assumed an object's color was a fixed, unchanging property

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：教授把学院传统与印象派对比，说学院把颜色当作物体固有、永久的属性——即固定不变的。A是reversed_logic，'颜色产生于眼中'恰恰是印象派的观点，与学院相反。B是topic_adjacent，反复在不同时刻画同一场景是莫奈的做法，不是学院传统。C也是reversed_logic/混淆，把光线当作颜色来源是印象派的洞见，不是学院的看法。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: how social facilitation affects performance on simple vs complex tasks
items.transcript: Have you ever noticed that you can ride a bike faster when someone's racing beside you, but you fumble a new card trick the moment people start watching? That contrast is the heart of a phenomenon social psychologists call social facilitation. The basic idea is simple. The mere presence of other people changes how we perform. But here's the twist that makes it interesting. The presence of others doesn't always help. It depends on the task. Back in eighteen ninety-eight, a researcher named Norman Triplett noticed that cyclists rode faster when competing against others than when racing alone against the clock. He tested this in the lab, asking children to reel in a fishing line as fast as they could. They worked faster with another child present. So an audience boosts performance, right? Not so fast. Later studies found the opposite for difficult tasks. When people tackled complex math problems or learned an unfamiliar skill, an audience actually made them worse. Psychologist Robert Zajonc resolved the puzzle in nineteen sixty-five. His explanation was elegant. The presence of others increases physiological arousal, and arousal strengthens our most likely, or dominant, response. For an easy, well-practiced task, the dominant response is the correct one, so we shine. For a hard, unfamiliar task, the dominant response is often a mistake, so we stumble. This is why a skilled pianist plays beautifully before a crowd, while a beginner falls apart. The same audience, the same arousal, but opposite outcomes depending on skill. Next week, we'll look at what happens when people work not in front of a group, but as part of one, and why individual effort sometimes mysteriously shrinks in that situation.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How competition among cyclists improves their racing times
options.B: How the presence of others affects performance differently by task
options.C: Why physiological arousal always harms careful problem solving
options.D: How children learn new skills faster than adults do

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座主旨是他人在场如何影响表现，并且对简单任务和复杂任务影响相反。A太狭窄，自行车只是一个早期例子。C是reversed_logic且过度——唤醒并非总是有害，对简单任务反而有帮助。D是wrong_topic，讲座没有比较儿童和成人的学习速度。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Norman Triplett find in his experiments?

options.A: Children solved complex math problems much worse with an audience
options.B: Cyclists rode more slowly whenever others were racing beside them
options.C: Children reeled a fishing line faster with another child present
options.D: Adults learned unfamiliar new skills much better when working alone

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座明确说Triplett让儿童尽快收钓鱼线，有另一个孩子在场时他们做得更快。A是wrong_detail，复杂数学题变差是后来研究的发现，不是Triplett的实验。B是reversed_logic，他发现骑手有人竞争时更快而非更慢。D是topic_adjacent，听起来合理但不是Triplett的发现。

questions.stem: Why does the professor contrast a skilled pianist with a beginner?

options.A: To restate that the presence of an audience increases physiological arousal
options.B: To suggest that nervous beginners should never perform in public
options.C: To prove that skilled pianists experience much less arousal than others
options.D: To illustrate how one audience produces opposite outcomes by skill

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：钢琴家与初学者的对比是为了说明同样的观众、同样的唤醒，却因技能水平不同而产生相反的结果。A是literal_restatement，只是重复了'观众增加唤醒'这一内容，没解释举例的目的。B是over_inference，教授没有给出初学者不该公开表演的建议。C是wrong_purpose，教授没说钢琴家唤醒更少，恰恰是同样的唤醒。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: Why people's individual effort can shrink within a group
options.B: How cyclists can train to ride faster against the clock
options.C: Why audiences make complex math problems easier to solve
options.D: How Zajonc measured arousal levels in laboratory animals

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：教授在结尾预告下周讨论人们作为群体一员工作时、个人努力为何有时神秘地减少。B是too_narrow，把话题局限在本课的自行车细节上。C是reversed_logic，本课说观众让复杂任务变难而非变易，且不是下次主题。D是wrong_topic，教授没提到要讲Zajonc如何在动物身上测量唤醒。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: Think about the last vacation you took. When you look back on it, do you replay every single hour? Of course not. Your memory keeps just a few highlights and skips the rest. Psychologists have a name for the pattern behind this: the peak-end rule. The idea is that when we judge a past experience, we don't average all the moments together. Instead, we remember it based mainly on two things: how it felt at its most intense moment, the peak, and how it felt at the very end. Everything in between gets surprisingly little weight. Daniel Kahneman demonstrated this with a now-famous study involving a mildly painful medical procedure. One group had a shorter procedure that ended at its most painful point. A second group had a longer procedure, with all the same pain, but the doctors added a few extra minutes of much milder discomfort at the end before stopping. Logically, the second group endured more total pain because their procedure lasted longer. Yet they remembered the whole experience as less unpleasant. Why? Because the ending was gentler. The gentle finish reshaped the entire memory. This has real consequences. It helps explain why a wonderful dinner can be ruined by a rude waiter at the very end, or why a difficult hike feels worthwhile if it finishes with a stunning view. Our remembering self and our experiencing self simply don't agree. So if you want a memory to feel good later, pay close attention to how things peak, and especially to how they end.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why medical procedures should always be made shorter
options.B: How people calculate the average of all their feelings
options.C: A rule explaining how we remember past experiences
options.D: How vacations create more memories than ordinary days

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座主旨是peak-end rule，一个解释我们如何记住过往经历的规则。A是too_narrow且是误读，缩短手术不是讲座的主张。B是reversed_logic，讲座明确说我们并不是把所有时刻平均起来。D是wrong_topic，度假只是开头的引子，不是关于度假制造更多记忆。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the peak-end rule say we base a memory on?

options.A: The total amount of time the whole experience lasted
options.B: The average of every moment within the experience
options.C: Only the very first moment when the experience began
options.D: The most intense moment and how the experience ended

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座明确说我们主要根据两件事来评判经历——最强烈的时刻（peak）和结尾的感受。A是topic_adjacent/wrong_detail，总时长恰恰是被低估的；讲座说更长的手术（更多总痛苦）反而记得没那么糟。B是reversed_logic，讲座说我们并不是取平均。C是wrong_detail，重点是结尾而非开头。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Kahneman's medical procedure study?

options.A: To give evidence that a gentler ending improves the memory
options.B: To recommend that doctors lengthen painful procedures
options.C: To prove that physical pain cannot be measured accurately
options.D: To show that the two groups felt identical pain throughout

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：教授描述Kahneman的研究是为了提供证据，证明更温和的结尾会改善对整段经历的记忆（第二组总痛苦更多却记得没那么糟）。B是over_inference，教授没建议医生延长痛苦手术。C是wrong_purpose，研究并非要证明疼痛无法测量。D是literal_restatement且有误，重点是结尾不同导致记忆不同，而非两组全程感受相同。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did the second group remember less unpleasantness?

options.A: Because their procedure was the shortest of the two groups
options.B: Because their procedure ended with milder discomfort
options.C: Because they were given pain medication before the procedure
options.D: Because they experienced less total pain than the first group

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座说第二组在结尾多了几分钟温和得多的不适才停止，正是这个更温和的结尾重塑了整段记忆。A是wrong_detail，第二组手术更长而非更短。C是topic_adjacent，讲座没提到术前止痛药。D是reversed_logic，第二组总痛苦其实更多，不是更少。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how coral reefs respond to ocean acidification
items.transcript: When we talk about threats to coral reefs, most people picture warming water and bleaching. But there's a quieter threat that works on the chemistry of the ocean itself: ocean acidification. Here's what's happening. The ocean absorbs a large share of the carbon dioxide we release into the atmosphere. When that carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater, it forms carbonic acid, and that lowers the water's pH, making it more acidic. Now, why should a coral care about pH? Corals are tiny animals that build skeletons out of calcium carbonate, the same mineral found in chalk and seashells. To build those skeletons, they need carbonate ions dissolved in the water. The trouble is that more acidic water contains fewer available carbonate ions. So as the ocean acidifies, corals have to spend more energy to pull together the building blocks for their skeletons. In laboratory experiments, scientists have grown corals in tanks adjusted to the more acidic conditions predicted for the year twenty one hundred. Those corals built thinner, weaker skeletons and grew more slowly than corals in normal seawater. A weaker skeleton matters enormously, because it's the reef's physical structure that shelters fish and buffers coastlines from storms. And here's the part that worries scientists most. Acidification doesn't act alone. It strikes the very same reefs that are already stressed by rising temperatures. A coral weakened by acidification is far less able to bounce back after a bleaching event. The two pressures combine, and the reef's ability to recover shrinks. So understanding this chemistry isn't just academic. It tells us why some reefs may be running out of time.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How warming water causes coral reefs to bleach
options.B: How chalk and seashells are formed in the ocean
options.C: How fish use coral reefs as shelter from storms
options.D: How ocean acidification weakens coral reef skeletons

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座主旨是海洋酸化如何削弱珊瑚的骨骼。A是wrong_topic，升温与白化只是开头用来对比的另一个威胁。B是surface_word_trap，用了chalk、seashells等词，但那只是解释碳酸钙的类比。C是too_narrow，鱼把珊瑚当庇护所只是说明骨骼重要性的一个细节。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens when carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater?

options.A: It forms carbonic acid and lowers the water's pH
options.B: It increases the number of available carbonate ions
options.C: It immediately raises the temperature of the surrounding water
options.D: It causes corals to build thicker, stronger skeletons

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：讲座明确说二氧化碳溶于海水形成碳酸，从而降低水的pH值。B是reversed_logic，酸化使可用碳酸根离子更少而非更多。C是topic_adjacent/wrong_detail，溶解二氧化碳讲的是化学反应，不是直接升温。D是reversed_logic，酸性条件下珊瑚骨骼更薄更弱，不是更厚更强。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention chalk and seashells?

options.A: To argue that seashells dissolve faster than coral skeletons
options.B: To help students picture the mineral corals use to build skeletons
options.C: To list other organisms that are harmed by acidification
options.D: To explain how scientists measure the pH of seawater

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：教授提到粉笔和贝壳是为了帮学生形象理解珊瑚用来造骨骼的矿物质——碳酸钙。A是over_inference，教授没说贝壳比珊瑚骨骼溶解得快。C是wrong_purpose，那不是在列举受酸化伤害的其他生物。D是wrong_purpose，与测量海水pH无关。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about reefs facing both acidification and warming?

options.A: Warming actually protects acidified corals from any further damage
options.B: Acidification truly matters only inside laboratory tank experiments
options.C: The combined pressures make recovery much harder for reefs
options.D: Most reefs can quite easily withstand both threats together

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：教授暗示两种压力叠加，珊瑚在白化后更难恢复，恢复能力被压缩。A是reversed_logic，升温不是保护而是加重伤害。B是topic_adjacent且错误，酸化的影响发生在真实海洋，实验只是模拟。D是over_generalization，与讲座担忧的语气相反——一些珊瑚可能时日无多。
